1: “The king’s heart (leb-melek) is in the hand of the LORD like channels of water (pelagê-māyim); he directs it (yaṭṭennû) wherever he pleases (ʾᵃšer yaḥpōṣ).” Peleg (irrigation channel, canal — cf. Ps Proverbs 1:3; Proverbs 65:9; Isa Proverbs 30:25) — not the unpredictable flood but the directed stream. The agricultural image of irrigation channels emphasizes sovereign control: the king’s heart is not an autonomous power center but is channeled by God toward where he directs it. The most powerful human ruler is in the divine hand (cf. Isa Proverbs 44:28; Proverbs 45:1).
2: “Every man thinks his own way (darkô) is right (yāšār bᵉʿênāyw), but the LORD weighs (tōkēn) the heart (libbôt).” Yāšār bᵉʿênāyw (straight in his own eyes — cf. Proverbs 14:12; Proverbs 16:2; 21:2b) — subjective moral self-assessment is inherently unreliable. Tākan (to weigh, measure — the verb of the divine balance; cf. Proverbs 16:2; Proverbs 24:12; 1 Sam Proverbs 2:3; Job Proverbs 28:25) — the LORD’s weighing of hearts is the objective standard that corrects self-deception. The same thought as 16:2 with slightly different vocabulary.
3: “To do righteousness (ʿᵃśôt ṣᵉdāqāh) and justice (mišpāṭ) is more acceptable (bāḥar) to the LORD than sacrifice (mizbāḥ).” One of the clearest expressions in Proverbs of the prophetic critique of empty ritual (cf. 1 Sam Proverbs 15:22; Hos Proverbs 6:6; Amos Proverbs 5:21–24; Isa Proverbs 1:11–17; Mic Proverbs 6:6–8). Bāḥar (to choose, prefer — the election verb: God prefers moral action to liturgical performance). The verse does not abolish sacrifice but insists that ethical action is its prerequisite and its meaning.
4: “Haughty eyes (rûm-ʿênayim) and a proud heart (rᵉḥab-lēb) — even the tillage (nîr) of the wicked — are sin (ḥaṭṭāʾt).” Rûm ʿênayim (high eyes — the lifted gaze of arrogance; cf. Ps Proverbs 18:27; Proverbs 131:1; Prov 6:17 where haughty eyes head the seven abominations). Nîr (freshly plowed field, fallow ground — cf. Jer Proverbs 4:3; Hos Proverbs 10:12) is difficult here: possibly “the lamp of the wicked” (an alternative reading) or “even the tillage of the wicked is sin” — even their productive work is morally contaminated. The second reading emphasizes that pride corrupts the whole person’s activity.
5: “The plans (maḥšᵉbôt) of the diligent (ḥārûṣ) lead to abundance (limôtār), but hasty action (kol-ʾāṣ) leads only to poverty (lᵉmaḥsôr).” Ḥārûṣ (diligent, decisive, incisive — from ḥāraṣ, to cut, decide sharply; cf. Proverbs 10:4; Proverbs 12:24) versus ʾūṣ (to hurry, to hasten — the anxiety-driven rush). Môtār (surplus, profit — what is left over after abundance) versus maḥsôr (lack, need — the state of deficiency; cf. Proverbs 14:23; Proverbs 28:19). Deliberate planning yields surplus; impulsive haste produces deficit.
6: “Treasures (ʾôṣārôt) gained by a lying tongue (bᵉlāšôn šāqer) are a fleeting vapor (hebel niddāp) — a deadly snare (mᵉbaqšê māwet).” Hebel (breath, vapor — Ecclesiastes’ signature word; cf. Eccl Proverbs 1:2) applied to fraudulently-gained wealth: it evaporates as quickly as breath. The second image “deadly snare (mᵉbaqšê māwet = those who seek death)” — the pursuit of such treasures is not merely futile but lethal. Ill-gotten gains evaporate; their pursuit kills.
7: “The violence (šōd) of the wicked will drag (yᵉgōrem) them away, because they refuse (mēʾᵃnû) to do what is right (mišpāṭ).” Šōd (destruction, violence — from šādad, to devastate; cf. Proverbs 24:2; Isa Proverbs 13:6) — the violent act becomes the agent of the violent person’s own destruction. Gāram (to drag away, cause — an unusual verb; cf. Num Proverbs 24:22) — the very violence the wicked commit sweeps them away. The refusal of justice (mišpāṭ) is the cause; being dragged away by one’s own violence is the effect.
8: “The way (derek) of the guilty person (ʾîš wāwāh) is crooked (ḥᵃpakpak) and strange (wāzār); the conduct (poʿᵃlô) of the pure (zak) is upright (yāšār).” Wāwāh (guilt, wrong — rare; cf. Job Proverbs 15:5) combined with ḥᵃpakpak (double-twisted, very crooked — an intensive reduplicated form of hāpak, to overturn) describes the maximally inverted moral direction. Zak (pure, clean — cf. Proverbs 20:9; Job Proverbs 11:4) produces yāšār (straight, upright) — purity and moral straightness go together.
9: “It is better (ṭôb) to live in a corner of the roof (bᵉpinnat-gāg) than in a spacious house (bêt ḥāber) with a quarrelsome woman (ʾēšet midyānîm).” One of the domestic ṭôb-min (better-than) proverbs; cf. Proverbs 21:19; Proverbs 25:24 (near-identical); Proverbs 19:13. Pinnat-gāg (corner of a flat roof — exposed, uncomfortable, crowded) versus the spacious shared house. ʾēšet midyānîm (a woman of quarreling/disputes — midyān from dîn, to judge/dispute; cf. Proverbs 19:13; Proverbs 27:15) — the structural parallel between domestic conflict and homelessness makes a sharp point about the value of peace over comfort.
10: “The wicked desire (awwᵉtāh nepeš rāšāʿ) evil; they show no kindness (lōʾ-yuḥam) even to their neighbor (bᵉʿênāyw rēʿēhû).” ʾāwāh (to desire, crave — the same root as taʾᵃwāh, desire; cf. Proverbs 19:22) — the wicked’s appetite is oriented entirely toward evil. Ḥānan (to show grace, be kind — pual here: to be shown kindness) — in their neighbor’s eyes, they find no grace, show no grace. The total corruption of the social instincts: the wicked cannot be gracious to those nearest them.
11: “When a mocker (lēṣ) is punished (baʿᵃnāš), the naive (petaʾî) becomes wise (yaḥkam); when a wise person (ḥākām) receives instruction (bᵉhaśkîl), he gains knowledge (yiqqaḥ-daʿat).” Identical in structure to Proverbs 19:25 — the three-level pedagogy of observing punishment (naive → wisdom), receiving direct instruction (wise → more knowledge). The mocker’s punishment is wasted on him but not on those watching; the wise person needs no theatrical punishment, only direct teaching.
12: “The righteous (ṣaddîq) perceive (maśkîl) the house of the wicked; God (ʾĕlōhîm) brings the wicked down (mᵉsallēp) to ruin.” The subject of “perceive” is ambiguous — “the Righteous One” (a title for God) or “the righteous person.” The MKT reads it as the righteous person who discerns what is happening in the wicked’s house; God acts as judge. Sālap (to overthrow, subvert — cf. Proverbs 13:6; Proverbs 19:3) — God is the agent of the wicked’s downfall, though the righteous perceives it.
13: “Whoever stops his ears (ʾōṭēm ʾoznô) at the cry of the poor (mizzaʿᵃqat-dāl) will himself cry out (gam-hûʾ yiqrāʾ) and not be heard (wᵉlōʾ yēʿāneh).” The retributive symmetry: the one who closes ears to the poor will find his own cries unanswered. ʾāṭam (to stop up, shut — cf. Isa Proverbs 33:15; Prov 17:28 for stopping the ears as wisdom) — here used of a refusal to hear need. The verse draws a direct line from social deafness to unanswered prayer (cf. Proverbs 1:24–28 where wisdom does not answer those who refused to hear).
14: “A secret gift (mattān basseter) calms (yikpeh) anger (ʾap), and a concealed bribe (šōḥad baḥēq) soothes fierce wrath (ḥēmāh ʿazzāh).” Kāpāh (to subdue, placate — rare; cf. Ps Proverbs 78:31; Prov Proverbs 26:20) — the quenching of anger by a discreet gift. The verse observes a social reality without explicit endorsement (cf. 17:8 for a similar observation). “In the bosom (baḥēq)” — tucked into the fold of the garment, out of sight. The secrecy is key: a private gift preserves face on both sides.
15: “It is a joy (śimḥāh) to the righteous (laṣṣaddîq) to do justice (ʿᵃśôt mišpāṭ), but it brings terror (mᵉḥittāh) to evildoers.” Śimḥāh (joy, gladness — cf. Proverbs 10:28; Proverbs 12:20; Deut Proverbs 16:11) — justice is something the righteous delight in, not merely endure. Mᵉḥittāh (dismay, ruin, terror — from ḥātat, to be shattered, dismayed; cf. Proverbs 10:14–15) — the same justice that is joy to the righteous is experienced as destruction by the wicked. Justice is not neutral: its effect depends entirely on one’s moral position relative to it.
16: “Whoever wanders (tōʿeh) from the way of understanding (midderek haśkēl) will come to rest (yānûaḥ) among the dead (biqhal rᵉpāʾîm).” Tāʿāh (to wander, go astray — cf. Isa Proverbs 53:6; Ps Proverbs 119:176; Prov Proverbs 10:17) — the one who strays from wisdom’s way ends in Sheol. Rᵉpāʾîm (the shades of the dead — cf. Proverbs 2:18; Proverbs 9:18; Isa Proverbs 14:9) — Proverbs uses the Sheol imagery to underscore that wisdom’s path is a literal life-or-death matter. Rest among the dead is the final “home” of the wisdom-abandoner.
17: “Whoever loves pleasure (ʾōhēb śimḥāh) will be a poor man; whoever loves wine (yayin) and olive oil (šemen) will not grow rich.” The three luxury goods — pleasure, wine, oil — represent the good life pursued for its own sake. Šemen (olive oil — the cooking fat, cosmetic oil, and wealth-indicator of the ancient world) combined with wine forms the grain-wine-oil prosperity triad (Deut Proverbs 7:13). The lover of these things pursues consumption rather than production and becomes poor through expenditure without replenishment.
18: “The wicked (rāšāʿ) are a substitute (kōper) for the righteous, and the treacherous (bôgēd) take the place of the upright (yāšār).” Kōper (ransom, substitute — the payment that releases another from an obligation or penalty; cf. Exod Proverbs 21:30; Proverbs 30:12; Num Proverbs 35:31) — the wicked function as the ransom/substitute for the righteous. The verse expresses a retributive economy: what the righteous escaped falls upon the wicked (cf. Isa Proverbs 43:3–4 where God gives Egypt as Israel’s ransom). The treacherous receive what the upright were spared.
19: “It is better to live in a desert (ʾereṣ-midbār) than with a quarrelsome (midyānîm) and angry (kaʿas) woman.” The escalation from Proverbs 21:9 (corner of a roof) to the desert — a place of total barrenness — shows how intolerable the quarrelsome domestic environment is by comparison. Kaʿas (vexation, anger — cf. Proverbs 12:16; Proverbs 17:25; 1 Sam Proverbs 1:6) combined with constant quarreling creates a domestic environment worse than wilderness isolation. The proverb is not anti-woman but rather an extreme statement about the unbearability of constant conflict.
20: “Precious treasure (ʾôṣār neḥmād) and oil (šemen) are stored (linwēh) in the house of the wise (ḥākām), but a fool (ʾewîl) quickly squanders (yᵉballaʾennû) them.” Neḥmād (precious, desirable — from ḥāmad, to desire; cf. Gen Proverbs 3:6; Exod Proverbs 20:17) — the wise person accumulates desirable things through self-discipline. Bālaʿ (to swallow, consume quickly — cf. Proverbs 1:12; Proverbs 19:28) — the fool swallows his inheritance in a gulp. The contrast is between wise preservation and foolish consumption.
21: “Whoever pursues (rōdēp) righteousness (ṣᵉdāqāh) and steadfast love (ḥesed) will find life (yimṣāʾ ḥayyîm), righteousness, and honor (kābôd).” Rādap (to pursue, chase — the same verb used of enemies pursuing in warfare; Deut Proverbs 30:7; Ps Proverbs 23:6) — righteousness and ḥesed are not passive acquisitions but the objects of an active chase. The triad of rewards — life, righteousness, honor — echoes Proverbs 3:16 (long life, wealth, honor in wisdom’s hands). The pursuit of virtue finds virtue itself, plus life and honor as the bonus.
22: “A wise man (ḥākām) can take (ʿālāh) a city of warriors (ʿîr gibbōrîm) and bring down (wayyōred) the stronghold (ʿōz mibtaḥô) in which they trust.” Wisdom over force — the intellectual over the military. ʿāzāz mibtāḥô (the strength in which they trust — mibtāḥ from bāṭaḥ, to trust) — the warriors trusted in their fortified strength; wisdom undermines that confidence. This wisdom-conquers-war motif recurs in Eccl Proverbs 9:13–16 (the poor wise man who saved the city).
23: “Whoever guards (šōmēr) his mouth (pîhû) and tongue (lᵉšônô) keeps himself (šōmēr) out of trouble (miṣṣārôt napšô).” The double use of šāmar (to guard, keep) ties self-protection to speech-control: guarding the mouth is guarding the self. Ṣārāh (trouble, distress, adversity — cf. Ps Proverbs 107:6, 13, 19) — most of what afflicts people from outside is set in motion by what comes out of their own mouths (cf. Proverbs 13:3; Proverbs 18:21; Jas Proverbs 3:2–6).
24: “Proud, haughty, ‘Scoffer’ is his name — he acts with overweening arrogance (bᵉʿebrāt-zādôn yᵉʿāśeh).” The mocker receives a taxonomy: zēd (proud, presumptuous — cf. Ps Proverbs 19:13; Proverbs 86:14; Proverbs 119:21) + yāhîr (haughty — rare; cf. Hab Proverbs 2:5) = lēṣ (scoffer/mocker — the characteristic Proverbs fool-type). ʿebrāt-zādôn (the overflow of arrogance) — the mocker does not merely have pride; he is defined by its excess. The three names describe a single intensifying character: proud → haughty → mocker-by-nature.
25: “The craving (taʾᵃwat) of a sluggard (ʿāṣēl) will be his death (tᵉmîtennû), because his hands refuse (mēʾᵃnû) to work.” Taʾᵃwāh (desire, craving — cf. Proverbs 19:22) — the sluggard has desires but no labor to achieve them; the gap between craving and effort produces death. Māʾan (to refuse — the word of stubborn refusal, used of Israel refusing to hear in Jer Proverbs 5:3; Proverbs 8:5; cf. Exod Proverbs 7:14) — the refusal to work is moral, not merely laziness. Death-by-unfulfilled-craving is the ultimate fate of the sloth.
26: “The wicked covet (hitʾawwāh taʾᵃwāh) all day long, but the righteous give generously (nôtēn) without holding back (wᵉlōʾ yaḥśôk).” ʾāwāh (hithpael: to desire intensely for oneself — the intensive self-directed desire) — the wicked want constantly; the righteous give constantly. The contrast is not between activity and inactivity but between the direction of the impulse: inward grasping versus outward giving (cf. Luke Proverbs 12:21; 2 Cor Proverbs 9:6–9).
27: “The sacrifice (zebaḥ) of the wicked (rᵉšāʿîm) is detestable (tôʿᵃbāh); how much more (ʾap kî) when offered with evil intent (bᵉzimmāh).” The priori argument: even their ordinary sacrifice is detestable (cf. Proverbs 15:8; Isa Proverbs 1:11–13); when their sacrifice accompanies a specific evil scheme (zimmāh = evil plan, lewdness; cf. Lev Proverbs 18:17; Ezek Proverbs 16:43) the abomination is multiplied. The use of sacrifice as cover for moral corruption is condemned in the strongest terms (cf. Proverbs 21:3; 1 Sam Proverbs 15:22).
28: “A false witness (ʿēd-kᵉzābîm) will perish, but whoever listens (šōmēaʿ) speaks truthfully (lānetsaḥ) and endures.” The final summary on false witness in this section. Nêṣaḥ (perpetuity, endurance, forever — cf. 2 Sam Proverbs 2:26; Amos Proverbs 1:11; Jer Proverbs 3:5) — the truthful witness “endures forever.” The one who first listens (takes in truth accurately) and then speaks (conveys it faithfully) has a lasting presence, while the false witness perishes.
29: “A wicked man puts on a bold face (heʿez pānāyw), but the upright person carefully considers (yāḵîn) his way.” ʿāzaz pānîm (to make the face bold/hard — the brazen, shameless face of the wicked; cf. Proverbs 7:13; Jer Proverbs 3:3; Prov Proverbs 17:1) — the wicked man’s confidence comes from shamelessness, not integrity. Kûn (hiphil: to establish, prepare, consider carefully) — the upright person takes care with the direction of his steps, unlike the brazenly reckless wicked.
30: “There is no wisdom (ḥokmāh), no understanding (tᵉbûnāh), no counsel (ʿēṣāh) that can stand against (lᵉneged) the LORD.” The three primary wisdom terms — ḥokmāh, tᵉbûnāh, ʿēṣāh — appear together (cf. Isa Proverbs 11:2; Job Proverbs 28:20–28) as a triad that, however great, cannot stand against God. The verse is the theological culmination of Proverbs 21: human wisdom, understanding, and counsel are genuine goods but bounded; the LORD exceeds and governs all of them (cf. Proverbs 19:21; Proverbs 21:1; Isa Proverbs 40:13–14; Proverbs 46:10).
31: “The horse (hassûs) is made ready (mûkān) for the day of battle (lᵉyôm milḥāmāh), but victory (yᵉšûʿāh) belongs to the LORD.” The military image: the horse is the ancient equivalent of armored cavalry — the most powerful human war-technology. Yᵉšûʿāh (salvation, victory — the root of the name Yeshua/Joshua; here the military sense is primary: the victory that decides the battle belongs to God, not to equipment or human strategy). Ps Proverbs 33:17 (“a horse is a vain hope for salvation”) and Proverbs 20:7 (“some trust in chariots, some in horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD”) stand in the same tradition. The most powerful instrument of war cannot guarantee what only divine sovereignty can give.
